summary:
The Jacobite Risings (1688–1746 AD) were a series of uprisings in Britain and Ireland supporting the claims of the exiled Catholic Stuart dynasty to the British throne — from the deposed James II and his son James Francis Edward Stuart ('the Old Pretender') to his grandson Charles Edward Stuart ('Bonnie Prince Charlie' or 'the Young Pretender') — driven by a combination of dynastic legitimism, Catholic-Anglican religious conflict, Scottish Highland clan loyalism, and opposition to the [[event:2365|Act of Union]] with England. The Jacobite cause drew its strongest support from the Scottish Highlands and Catholic Ireland, though it also attracted English non-jurors (Anglicans who refused to swear oaths to William III and subsequent monarchs).

The major risings were: the 1689–1691 uprising (the 'Glencoe Massacre' — the 1692 murder of MacDonald clansmen by government forces — became a symbol of anti-Jacobite brutality); the 1715 rising ('the Fifteen'), which was poorly managed and collapsed at the Battle of Sheriffmuir; and the 1745 rising ('the Forty-Five'), which came closest to success. Charles Edward Stuart landed in Scotland in July 1745, rallied Highland clans, and marched deep into England as far as Derby — within 125 miles of London — before his officers insisted on retreat due to lack of English Jacobite support. The Jacobite army fell back to Scotland and was decisively destroyed at the [[event:2385|Battle of Culloden]] (April 16, 1746), after which government forces systematically disarmed the Highlands and dismantled the clan system. Bonnie Prince Charlie escaped to France and eventually died in Rome in 1788, ending the Stuart cause.
